title: The Publishing Houses of Nashville
summary: Interviewed by Taylor Branch at his Atlanta home in March 2011 for the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture, C.T. Vivian opens with why the Sunday-school publishing houses settled in Nashville, then turns to his own birth in Howard County, Missouri, and his family's move to Illinois so he could attend a non-segregated school.

title: A Fire Under My Bedroom
summary: Vivian tells how his grandmother, a Church of God in Christ woman, took in a woman a common-law husband was putting into the snow, and how the angered man set a fire in the coal cellar under Vivian's bedroom in the night, his grandmother screaming his name until he ran out moments before the room collapsed into the fire.

title: Two Women and a College Town
summary: Vivian describes the Depression destroying the family's three farms and prompting divorces among his mother and grandmother, and the two women deciding in 1930 to move to Macomb, Illinois, chosen because it had a university, so that the six-year-old could one day go to college, the dream his grandmother fulfilled through him.

title: A Sit-In in Peoria
summary: Vivian recounts going back to Peoria to work and return to school, rooming with chemist Ben Alexander, and joining a church group's direct action using techniques from CORE, the Congress of Racial Equality founded by Jim Farmer in 1941, to open restaurants and lunch counters in Peoria in 1947, years before Greensboro.

title: Too Nice to Let You Sit Alone
summary: Vivian tells how he met his wife Octavia at a party welcoming her as women's and girls' director at Carver Community Center, the largest African American center between Chicago and St. Louis, and how he called to take her out for her February birthday, after which the two were inseparable.

title: From Pontiac to Ted's Trailer
summary: Vivian describes Octavia's upbringing in Pontiac, Michigan, where a doctor-led group of young people published a newspaper and registered voters, and her later role in a Peoria lunch-counter action at a drive-in called Ted's Trailer, where her pastor took young people inside to eat rather than accept bags at the car.

title: Up South
summary: Asked where his and Octavia's families came from, Vivian traces her people to rice-growing Arkansas and his to Missouri, where his grandfather was fired as a principal for teaching Black children algebra, and explains the phrase up south, the recognition that the North was only marginally better than the South.

title: He Refused His Owner's Name
summary: Vivian recounts his great-great-grandfather leaving slavery to join the northern armies and, on returning to Boonville, refusing to take his former owner's name, choosing his wife's name instead, and traces the family names Tyndall, Woods, and Vivian, including a search for Vivians as far as Vivian, Louisiana.

title: A Pastor's Savings and a Seminary
summary: Vivian explains that though he was set to attend Colgate Rochester after being picked among the hot 100, his call to the ministry in Peoria revealed his pastor had been saving money to send him to American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville, the turn that put him into the movement, where the leaders, Kelly Miller Smith of First Baptist and seminary students like Bernard Lafayette, Jim Bevel, and John Lewis, came from the religious side of life.

title: A Booming Voice Through the Roof
summary: Vivian recounts the moment of his call while crossing the floor at Helen Gallagher's mail-order company, hearing a booming voice through the ceiling say it wanted him to work eight or ten hours a day, and the strangeness that the packagers around him went on with their work as though they had heard nothing.

title: An African American Mail-Order House
summary: Vivian describes Helen Gallagher Foster's rooster-themed mail-order goods advertised in House Beautiful, and his own plan to start an African American mail-order house to reach Black customers with new postwar money before Johnson Publications did, a venture he dropped when the call to ministry came.

title: Whose Faith Is in Question Now
summary: Vivian tells how, the night he learned Octavia was expecting their first child, he resolved not to leave her in Peoria for seminary, until her words, whose faith is in question now, sent him to Nashville, and how she soon called to say she was coming to stay, and a job and a dorm room fell into place just in time.

title: Jim Lawson Comes to Town
summary: Vivian recounts that the son born in Nashville had cerebral palsy, that he and Octavia produced a twenty-four-page piece on King the publishing house feared printing, and that after Kelly Miller Smith gathered a handful of ministers, Jim Lawson arrived, anchored at Vanderbilt and working for the Fellowship of Reconciliation, bringing the training in nonviolence no one had yet provided.

title: Just Back From India
summary: Vivian recalls meeting Lawson, who lived across the street, and welcoming his deeper knowledge of nonviolence, and how Lawson, fresh from India, taught skeptical students for months, including Diane Nash, who, taught that government is a monopoly of violence, was horrified by the slapping exercises and wondered how nonviolence could work.

title: The Students of the Workshops
summary: Vivian remembers a Methodist minister burned by a cigarette who insisted they buy him a new suit, notes that many SCLC figures like Abernathy, Coretta, and Fred Shuttlesworth came out of Alabama, and recalls the students in Lawson's workshops, John Lewis, Jim Bevel, Bernard Lafayette, Diane Nash, a few seminary men not fully engaged, and the Southern Baptist who stood out among a handful of white students.

title: The President Who Wrote on Jesus
summary: Vivian reflects cautiously on hearing untouchables at a South Africa conference describe Gandhi's attitude toward them differently than Americans understood, and tells the irony that Jim Lawson was expelled from Vanderbilt by a president who had written the very book on the life of Jesus used in their seminary, a text the students saw as refuting the South.

part: Can You Be a Christian and a Racist
title: Killing Communists in Greensboro
summary: Vivian notes the Greensboro sit-ins began February 1, 1960, the night after King's farewell from Dexter, and that the Nashville group had been preparing for a year when Greensboro acted without preparation, recalling a later visit to Greensboro where a police chief showed no regret over killing people he called communists.

title: A Religious Confrontation
summary: Vivian argues that bringing segregation into the open as a religious confrontation in the Bible Belt changed everything, and describes his workshop method of asking whether one can be a Christian and a racist at the same time, keeping the rooms mostly white but with Black participants to answer the white doubters directly.

title: State the Scripture and They'd Look It Up
summary: Vivian contends the South believed in the Bible above theology, so that citing scripture on loving one's neighbor sent people home to verify and return convinced, and frames two movements running together: by any means necessary and nonviolent direct action with a biblical base founded on Jesus.

title: Look Them in the Eye
summary: Vivian discusses the workshop training to keep eye contact rather than curl into a fetal position, distinguishing SCLC's upright stance from SNCC's, recounts Fred Shuttlesworth's story of King keeping his pace as a car bore down on them, and sets the Nashville timeline toward the April 19 march as he listened to Lawson telephone a Greensboro leader troubled the students had sat down unprepared.

title: Alabama, Then Mississippi If Necessary
summary: Vivian explains why trained people were kept from the front lines and lays out a geographic strategy: SCLC chose Alabama, where the method came in Montgomery, the civil rights bill in Birmingham, and the voting rights bill in Selma, while SNCC took the youngest people into the hardest place, Mississippi, aiming to change towns rather than national law.

title: A Bombing During the Meeting
topic: SNCC's Formation and the Looby Bombing
summary: Vivian clarifies that SNCC formed at the Shaw meeting in Raleigh as the sit-ins spread and that John Lewis was leaving SNCC by Bloody Sunday, then recounts an early-morning leadership meeting at a church near Fisk when a distant blast first thought to be at Meharry sent Bernard Lafayette to Tennessee A&I to organize before they learned the home of attorney Z. Alexander Looby had been bombed.

title: Nothing but the Feet
summary: Vivian describes the march forming from Tennessee A&I after lunch, gathering Fisk and Pearl High School students, growing as people came off porches, and falling into a silent march into downtown where nothing could be heard but feet, a technique he says had been pioneered in New York in the 1920s.

title: Backed Up Against the Buildings
summary: Vivian recalls factory workers playing catch who fell silent and backed against the walls as the marchers passed, the crowd swelling to four thousand though papers reported two thousand, and the procession reaching City Hall where he and Diane Nash awaited the mayor, who would have to answer her planned final question.

title: I Could Not Justify It
summary: Vivian recounts Mayor Ben West saying he would examine the matter, then opening everything in Nashville a week later, the televised exchange in which Diane Nash pressed him until he said he could not justify segregation, and a portrait of Z. Alexander Looby, the lead Black lawyer who had defended the arrested demonstrators, which is why the white community reacted to the bombing of his home.

title: Marching Through the Yellow Pages
summary: Vivian notes the mayor opened most places but not the theaters, recalls John Lewis's line about marching through the yellow pages, and describes returning from Denver electrified by footage of a leader struck and bleeding, explaining that because theaters open at night the campaign required night marches that gave attackers the cover of darkness and demanded extra commitment.

title: A Picnic Interrupted
summary: Vivian recalls a picnic celebrating the end of the theater campaign on Mother's Day 1961 when word came of buses burned and riders beaten in Anniston and Birmingham, the evening meeting led by Diane Nash where, agreeing to go to Jackson, some wept and ten students stood ready, and the restated principle that they would never let violence stop nonviolence.

title: The Militia on the Bus
summary: Vivian recounts an earlier Birmingham bus-station incident where a policeman leaned over him but did not have him removed, tied to the interstate-versus-intrastate distinction, then describes boarding in Montgomery with white passengers up front, state troopers filling the front rows with Alabama militia, a long trail of news cars to the state line, and letting Jim Lawson explain nonviolence to reporters.

title: Asking for Trouble and Getting It
summary: Vivian recalls a Mississippi colonel unfastening the speedometer and ordering the driver to barrel into Jackson, people waving silently from porches, the Life magazine framing asking for trouble and getting it, and how, arguing with Colonel Birdsong over a bathroom stop, he was told to stay on the back because they needed experience at the end, making him the last rider off in Jackson.

title: Whether He Would Get on the Bus
summary: Vivian recalls the riders besieged in Abernathy's Montgomery church until Robert Kennedy sent marshals, the meeting at pharmacist Richard Harris's home where Diane Nash asked King to ride and an argument arose over whether he would board, his own decision with Jim Lawson to continue, and patting the Jackson police captain on the back to say he was with them, perhaps the first ever to ask to go to jail.

title: You Promised Me Only One Bus
topic: The Second Bus and States' Rights
summary: Vivian recounts that Jim Farmer, who as CORE director launched the original adult ride, rejoined on the second bus, which was a terrible surprise to government, the Mississippi governor protesting to Robert Kennedy that he had been promised only one bus, and explains the stakes of interstate versus intrastate travel: the segregationists wanted segregation kept a state matter while the movement wanted federal enforcement.

title: Two People Delivering the Food
summary: Vivian describes the riders setting up a routine in the Jackson jail with meditation, strategy, and a Sunday service organized through the bars, stunning the guards by seating themselves and having two of their own deliver the food, winning the Black trustees' respect, and recounts Jim Bevel explaining that a lieutenant's rage came from Vivian looking him straight in the eye.

title: Modeled on a Plantation
summary: Vivian describes being moved early to Parchman, a farm-prison he calls one of the most vicious ever, modeled on a plantation with two burial grounds, and the questioning where, after he answered yes sir and no sir, the warden suddenly asked whether he had syphilis and four or five men jumped him.

title: As Soon as the Blood Spurted
summary: Vivian recounts warding off blows until the warden's embellished leather-and-steel blackjack cut him down the side, the men jumping back at the sight of blood, and how, led toward the cells, he felt something strange down his neck and turned at the steel gate to find the guard pointing a thirty-eight at his head, looking him in the eye until the guard lowered the gun and slammed the door.

title: Saved by the Blood
summary: Vivian recounts that a released white rider's words to the press brought Washington's calls and Kennedy's word that he had been beaten until he bled, which made the fearful warden dread removal, that Kelly Miller Smith arrived within hours to take him back to Nashville, and reflects on how local officials grew uncertain once Washington took notice, returning to the gun as one of a few truly religious experiences of his life.

title: Fingers Behind My Head
summary: Vivian considers how being a freedom rider shaped his identity, describes the crooked Parchman cellblock that funneled prisoners single file past the police, and recounts a second truly religious experience in an Alabama jail where he got his fingers behind his head just in time so a billy club struck them rather than his skull, an event he ties to the day of his confrontation with Sheriff Clark in Selma.

title: Let Me Hit Him
summary: Vivian describes a huge jailer taking him up in a small hand-operated elevator, a second large man asking let me hit him, and bringing his fingers together just as the club came down, then being shoved into a cell where he dropped to his knees in thanks not for surviving but for having done and said all the right things, noting the Selma confrontation sounds scripted yet came without any idea beforehand of what he would say.

title: Forty People up the Steps
summary: Vivian sets the courthouse confrontation on February 15, 1965, three weeks before Bloody Sunday, describing Sheriff Clark coming out and closing the door with his men ranged on the top step as about forty people marched up the bottom steps, and ties this mystery to the gun pulled on him in Parchman.

title: Take Their Money, Vote Like You Want
summary: Vivian explains how he became SCLC's director of affiliates, leaving Nashville to pastor in Chattanooga where a fellow minister had the voting statistics he needed, and describes winning three-fifths of the city council across three elections, including one rainy day, with a strategy against the bought Black vote: take the buyer's money but vote your conscience, illustrated by a man who pocketed the pay yet voted for whom he chose.

title: A Name for All the Things
summary: Vivian recounts that his Chattanooga and statewide voter work led Dorothy Cotton to recommend him to King, who through Wyatt Tee Walker brought him to Atlanta as national director of affiliates, overseeing SCLC-affiliated groups funded from the national office, a role that made him a traveling troubleshooter and led him to give up his church.

title: The Necessary Thing
summary: Vivian recounts that his first national-office assignment was Birmingham, King's go-for-broke campaign launched at the 1962 convention with the warning that it took twenty-three thousand soldiers to enroll James Meredith at Ole Miss, and Wyatt Walker's plan called Project C for confrontation, and tells how he and Walker spotted government men on the telephone-company balconies and amused themselves making calls to watch them hurry inside.

title: The Dogs and the Hoses
summary: Vivian describes the May 3, 1963 tactic of sending students out the church front door toward the hoses while others slipped out the back to occupy downtown, so that Bull Connor found the city taken and the hoses pointless, beginning the deal, and how the dogs and hoses backfired, the image of ripped trousers and a lunging dog showing the world the brutality and drawing ministers from across the country.

title: The Silent March Against Lynching
summary: Vivian explains the 1920s silent march in New York organized by the NAACP and W.E.B. Du Bois against lynching, with banners and only the sound of feet, which the Nashville marchers consciously echoed downtown, and notes that on the Birmingham jail decision he defers to Dorothy Cotton, who tells it best.

title: Never Too Young to Fight
summary: Vivian recounts the argument over using young people, won by Jim Bevel, who held that one is never too young to fight for freedom, calling it the only children's movement he knows in nonviolent work, remembers urging high-school students who climbed out of locked windows to cross over Jordan, and tells how Birmingham parents who opposed their children marching were converted when the children went to jail.

part: St. Augustine and the Civil Rights Bill
title: Keeping the Legislation Moving
summary: Vivian declines a memory of the Birmingham church bombing funeral while affirming he was at Jimmie Lee Jackson's, treats a 1963 Richmond panel on nonviolence as one of many speeches, and argues St. Augustine's real purpose in 1964 was not the town but keeping the civil rights legislation moving through Congress and pressing Johnson, explaining why he, unlike Bevel, wanted to go.

title: A Human Net in the Ocean
summary: Vivian describes St. Augustine's 400th anniversary and its segregated 1600 feet of beach, where white swimmers would form a human net and drown anyone who walked in, and how, leading a wade-in with Fred Shuttlesworth who could not swim, they went in at an angle until one of the men jumped too soon.

title: I Got a Right to Be on This Ocean
summary: Vivian recounts a man jumping on top of him and driving his face into the sand as he gave himself up for dead, until a policeman jerked the man off and pulled him to the surface, and how, when the officer moved to arrest him, Vivian insisted on his right to the ocean until the attacker fled and the policeman, persuaded, brought him towels.

title: The Slave Market as a Classroom
summary: Vivian describes turning St. Augustine's old downtown slave market into a teaching center at noon for tourists eating lunch, and a midnight march he and Dorothy Cotton led where police cut them off, unknowingly from Hoss Manucy's men waiting with chains and bars, the night a voice shouted that they had no god.

title: Whether Anybody Goes or Not, I'm Going
summary: Vivian recounts a night march where attackers threw things from the beautiful walls and forty marchers were sent to the hospital, regrouping at a church where he declared he would go whether anyone followed and walked down the aisle until people rose behind him, and notes that King, Fred Shuttlesworth, and he went to jail in St. Augustine along with nineteen rabbis, after which came the landmark Civil Rights Act.

title: Not Just the Town
summary: Vivian explains that as national affiliates director he went to Selma to gauge how prepared people were and to secure an invitation, King having largely decided already, that Selma meant not just the town but the surrounding counties from which Black people came, and recalls a St. Augustine state legislator intimidated to tears with his windows knocked out before a planned meeting.

title: This Is the End of the Line
summary: Vivian recounts the controversial decision to take the movement north to Chicago, his readiness to leave after concluding upon winning Selma that the work must now train people in the locals, and a call from Jim Morton to lead the Ford Fellowship program at a new urban training center, which he accepted from a filling-station telephone while driving across Indiana, reaching Chicago before King did and disclaiming any role in the Meredith march.

title: Who Made What Happen
summary: Vivian recalls breakfasting at the Gaston Motel when word came that Meredith had been shot near Memphis, and gives his retrospective verdict that those who stayed with nonviolence won, judging movements by who made what happen, noting SNCC ended without students, nonviolence, or a committee for lack of a base while SCLC survived through the church.

title: The Prophet of Our Age
summary: Vivian recounts his first real conversation with King on the Fisk campus after the Montgomery victory, when King came for an honorary doctorate and Vivian was covering him for the Sunday-school publishing house, and how he concluded King could weather the storm beyond Montgomery, calling him the prophet of the age whose change was moral and spiritual.

title: A Must-Is, Not a Has-Been
summary: Vivian relays King's joke about a horse buyer wanting not a has-been or a will-be but a must-is, recalls that Abernathy could have led SCLC but gave it to King and settled disputes for him, and remembers King rocking on his heels singing There Is a Balm in Gilead, his founding a philosophical organization in college, and above all his understanding of timing.

title: Education Must Follow Freedom
summary: Asked about his legacy, Vivian says only that he wants to keep fighting the struggle on a multiplicity of fronts, and describes building an organization to produce up to a million Black students a year who pass the ACT at a high level, having concluded that education must always follow freedom.

title: The Hoses Did Not Turn On
summary: Vivian tells the story of Birmingham ministers leading their congregations straight from church to sing to the children in jail, meeting Bull Connor and a line of firemen with hoses, and how, after the people prayed and rose, the firemen got up and walked away and the hoses never turned on.

title: He Crawled Out Onto the Road
summary: Vivian closes on Charles Billups, who led the Red Sea March prayers, an immaculate salesman taken by nonviolence who, after Klansmen tied him to a tree and beat him, crawled onto a highway certain he would die until a car carried him to the hospital, and who, after King was shot, flew to the funeral with a suitcase holding nothing but ties.
related_events: assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
